title: Understand even and odd numbers visually
description: Use groups of objects to determine is a number is even or odd.
topic_path: Get ready for multiplication and division
Understand even and odd numbers visually

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an even number of flowers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. A vertical dotted line divides the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. A horizontal dotted line divides the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\maroonD5$ flowers.

![2 columns of flowers. Column 1 has 3 flowers. Column 2 has 2 flowers. A horizontal dotted line divides the flowers into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra flower.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1f9a181b8dcdf883dd3d539a571474520a1301a5)

Each flower does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of flowers.

In this image, there are $\maroonD9$ flowers.

![2 rows of flowers. Row 1 has 4 flowers. Row 2 has 5 flowers. A vertical dotted line divides the flowers into 4 columns so there are 4 pairs with 1 extra flower.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0d673af50eb46dae38880d2e544d80b3ba43ad06)

Each flower does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of flowers.

In this image, there are $\blueE6$ flowers.

![2 rows of flowers. Row 1 has 3 flowers. Row 2 has 3 flowers. A vertical dotted line divides the flowers into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ec0810fba4b6f1bbdce3636a88730e2ae17f8e47)

Each flower has a pair, so there are an even number of flowers.

This image shows an even number of flowers:

![2 rows of flowers. Row 1 has 3 flowers. Row 2 has 3 flowers. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/410d9c3ceea9080c5db8f989d698d39bab1e9cef)

choices.content: ![2 columns of flowers. Column 1 has 3 flowers. Column 2 has 2 flowers.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/47c7af5f3b790f54f78dffa89df187a35a840730)
![2 rows of flowers. Row 1 has 4 flowers. Row 2 has 5 flowers.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1ff322ccb063778c7141034db2b9fdcdeee60b15)
![2 rows of flowers. Row 1 has 3 flowers. Row 2 has 3 flowers.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/410d9c3ceea9080c5db8f989d698d39bab1e9cef)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an odd number of acorns?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. A vertical dotted line divides the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. A horizontal dotted line divides the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs and 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\blueE4$ acorns.

![2 columns of acorns. Column 1 has 2 acorns. Column 2 has 2 acorns. A vertical dotted line divides the acorns into 2 columns so there are 2 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6002ccb37b7a31edc664db844c9ed13a0ff6770e)

Each acorn has a pair, so there are an even number of acorns.

In this image, there are $\blueE8$ acorns.

![4 rows of acorns. Each row has 2 acorns. A horizontal dotted line divides the acorns into 4 rows so there are 4 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/33dfa54fe702ab1832880424e0cdd8624ccc3c19)

Each acorn has a pair, so there are an even number of acorns.

In this image, there are $\maroonD7$ acorns.

![2 rows of acorns. Row 1 has 3 acorns. Row 2 has 4 acorns. A vertical dotted line divides the acorns into 4 columns so there are 3 pairs with 1 extra acorn.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f65338279a4ec92200b4348060cc5c9aa32d70ee)

Each acorn does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of acorns.

This image shows an odd number of acorns:

![2 rows of acorns. Row 1 has 3 acorns. Row 2 has 4 acorns.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3adbd98f8f26ab8d1bee1bd310e33186c18df22f)

choices.content: ![2 rows of acorns. Row 1 has 3 acorns. Row 2 has 4 acorns.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3adbd98f8f26ab8d1bee1bd310e33186c18df22f)
![2 rows of acorns. Row 1 has 2 acorns. Row 2 has 2 acorns.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5fd1844c5b2c4d551ce18f42ff564dccc475732a)
![2 columns of acorns. Column 1 has 4 acorns. Column 2 has 4 acorns.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6c0fb9ebf381a38dd958a1dde2e35c406677ee06)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an even number of cheese triangles?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. A vertical dotted line divides the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. A horizontal dotted line divides the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\maroonD9$ cheese triangles.

![2 columns of cheese triangles. Column 1 has 5 cheese triangles. Column 2 has 4 cheese triangles. A horizontal dotted line divides the cheese triangles into 5 rows so there are 4 pairs with 1 extra cheese triangle.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f43b4da0a1cdc117dcb33e878cb9411e87c07af7)

Each cheese triangle does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of cheese triangles.

In this image, there are $\maroonD{11}$ cheese triangles.

![2 rows of cheese triangles. Row 1 has 5 cheese triangles. Row 2 has 6 cheese triangles. A vertical dotted line divides the cheese triangles into 6 columns so there are 5 pairs with 1 extra cheese triangle.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/27b48a7fdf2c958a0d8d5a83a4a7a92b3dd0f75a)

Each cheese triangle does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of cheese triangles.

In this image, there are $\blueE{10}$ cheese triangles.

![2 rows of cheese triangles. Row 1 has 5 cheese triangles. Row 2 has 5 cheese triangles. A vertical dotted line divides the cheese triangles into 5 columns so there are 5 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6c05692f73bd892e6f6cf75d47f8b461c2264f3d)

Each cheese triangle has a pair, so there are an even number of cheese triangles.

This image shows an even number of cheese triangles:

![2 rows of cheese triangles. Row 1 has 5 cheese triangles. Row 2 has 5 cheese triangles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5cad2b898ca9d8342467ea6144f3941b6c6d0de1)

![2 rows of cheese triangles. Row 1 has 5 cheese triangles. Row 2 has 5 cheese triangles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5cad2b898ca9d8342467ea6144f3941b6c6d0de1)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an odd number of bananas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. A vertical dotted line divides the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. A horizontal dotted line divides the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\blueE{16}$ bananas.

![4 rows of bananas. Each row has 4 bananas. A horizontal dotted line divides the bananas into groups of 2 rows and a vertical dotted line divides the bananas into 4 columns so there are 8 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9385229cbd986eaedbd5c5bec0435fba9a5fa261)

Each banana has a pair, so there are an even number of bananas.

In this image, there are $\blueE{12}$ bananas.

![2 rows of bananas. Each row has 6 bananas. A vertical dotted line divides the bananas into 6 columns so there are 6 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5a9fe351c0396f4231eea4952168e146c21c95c5)

Each banana has a pair, so there are an even number of bananas.

In this image, there are $\maroonD7$ bananas.

![2 rows of bananas. Row 1 has 3 bananas and Row 2 has 4 bananas.  A vertical dotted line divides the bananas into 4 columns so there are 3 pairs with 1 extra banana.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9b71cc2c3715401cd05ebff6775cd68472c43352)

Each banana does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of bananas.

This image shows an odd number of bananas:

![2 rows of bananas. Row 1 has 3 bananas. Row 2 has 4 bananas.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/47870d4e644f901011ca4758d35bf3696150cd25)

choices.content: ![4 rows of bananas. Each row has 4 bananas.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff227dffa161e51d82807c1a9e28ba38f471cb75)
![2 rows of bananas. Row 1 has 3 bananas. Row 2 has 4 bananas.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/47870d4e644f901011ca4758d35bf3696150cd25)
![2 rows of bananas.Row 1 has 6 bananas. Row 2 has 6 bananas.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9bdb45ec7b0df420c63d649c1869646914e9c441)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an even number of rabbits?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. A vertical dotted line divides the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. A horizontal dotted line divides the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\maroonD{17}$ rabbits.

![3 rows of rabbits. Row 1 has 5 rabbits. Row 2 has 6 rabbits. Row 3 has 6 rabbits. Horizontal dotted lines divide the rabbits into 3 rows and vertical dotted lines divide the rabbits into 3 columns. There are 8 pairs with 1 extra rabbit.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6d4112fd4133f4076c6a8964e8e60055002fecd)

Each rabbit does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of rabbits.

In this image, there are $\maroonD{5}$ rabbits.

![1 row of 5 rabbits. Vertical dotted lines divide the rabbits into 3 columns. There are 2 pairs with 1 extra rabbit.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b101063296381e476911c3afbe755303beb8201b)

Each rabbit does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of rabbits.

In this image, there are $\blueE{16}$ rabbits.

![4 rows of rabbits. Each row has 4 rabbits. A horizontal dotted line divides the rabbits into 2 rows and vertical dotted lines divide the rabbits into 4 columns. There are 8 pairs of rabbits.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a5c14c4e9fd9ad9833e8f8ab99e7b3a4c2b1bb5f)

Each rabbit has a pair, so there are an even number of rabbits.

This image shows an even number of rabbits:

![4 rows of rabbits. Each row has 4 rabbits.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7a8ee66ce4eacd3cc544a2b0fa477d8ca91ad376)

choices.content: ![3 rows of rabbits. Row 1 has 6 rabbits. Row 2 has 6 rabbits. Row 3 has 5 rabbits.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7fe481b9336eda00fdde6ff1a8a3d38f114c9c45)
![4 rows of rabbits. Each row has 4 rabbits.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7a8ee66ce4eacd3cc544a2b0fa477d8ca91ad376)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which image shows an odd number of apples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![2 rows of cookies. Row 1 has 3 cookies. Row 2 has 3 cookies. Vertical dotted lines divide the cookies into 3 columns so there are 3 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![2 columns of cookies. Column 1 has 3 cookies. Column 2 has 2 cookies. Horizontal dotted lines divide the cookies into 3 rows so there are 2 pairs with 1 extra cookie.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\blueE{14}$ apples.

![2 rows of apples. Row 1 has 7 apples. Row 2 has 7 cookies. Vertical dotted lines divide the cookies into 7 columns so there are 7 pairs.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5dede9bd017270ba0451eaeb2fcb5d494990de17)

Each apple has a pair, so there are an even number of apples.

In this image, there are $\blueE{18}$ apples.

![3 rows of apples. Each row has 6 apples. Vertical dotted lines divide the apples into 3 columns and horizontal dotted lines divide the apples into 3 rows. There are 9 pairs of apples.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9f7cf3d61992c348e4849d9245e3aa4b88c73b52)

Each apple has a pair, so there are an even number of apples.

In this image, there are $\maroonD{11}$ apples.

![2 columns of apples. Column 1 has 6 apples. Column 2 has 5 apples. Horizontal dotted lines divide the apples into 6 rows so there are 5 pairs of apples with 1 extra apple.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/442eb82a9ae37bd1dd66aed8986c3767762c5da0)

Each apple does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of apples.

This image shows an odd number of apples:

![2 columns of apples. Column 1 has 6 apples. Column 2 has 5 apples. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/42983aa23e9288ca21d6cdeb380f4b18dab5d8d6)

choices.content: ![2 rows of apples. Each row has 7 apples.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62d8a3f9d928ac340efe5912d505401437c3052e)
![2 columns of apples. Column 1 has 6 apples. Column 2 has 5 apples. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/42983aa23e9288ca21d6cdeb380f4b18dab5d8d6)
![3 rows of apples. Each row has 6 apples. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/96f5eccedfea9170161ef41470fc06d7ee8173c5)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Determine whether the number of objects is even or odd.**

Image | Even/Odd
- | - | -
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/89e0d9b412aeedb876f0f6885c904a5054bdde5d) | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf958c80fbfec114f3aeabc9a2c54d2db9803623) | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/591e2da531e474dd1dcc419d7bf818a27fd90f99)  | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints:
An $\blueE{\text{even}}$ number is a number that can be split into $2$ equal groups.

We find $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers by making sure every object in the group has a pair.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d50cd961bb1ee962eb60cc708a5e737e9d4486c)

Examples of $\blueE{\text{even}}$ numbers are $\blueE2, \blueE4,\blueE6, \blueE8, \blueE{10},  . .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

We find $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers by trying to pair objects and finding there is **not** always a pair.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/10c3af09ebfcd0c6d05b4edd219f33894cc4d8b9)

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\maroonD7$ pigs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b4fa182e48b6c60c3d1998258bc94d03fbfa05f0)

Each pig does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of pigs.

In this image, there are $\blueE6$ eggs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4550571e0d12949615f1617fbd913437f9db7658)

Each egg has a pair, so there are an even number of eggs.

In this image, there are $\maroonD3$ sharks.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7d7f96c886eed31d1e24590b1a8f17eafa033759)

Each shark does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of sharks.

Examples of $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ numbers are $\maroonD1, \maroonD3, \maroonD5, \maroonD7, \maroonD9, . . . $

In this image, there are $\blueE{4}$ hamburgers.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d4976d842e677f249cb6bc79d0685175cb4d5ee)

Each hamburger has a pair, so there are an even number of hamburgers.

In this image, there are $\blueE{20}$ carrots.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c258bfb2ae95d393e6eae1dd263638b232071a45)

Each carrot has a pair, so there are an even number of carrots.

In this image, there are $\maroonD{19}$ caterpillars.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/90baefe56e628ed0978ab77ef6c7e1bb5e670fc9)

Each caterpillar does **not** have a pair, so there are an odd number of caterpillars.
